title: Idempotency in Configuration Management
description:
## Why Re-running Is Safe

A core property of good configuration management is **idempotency**. An on-call engineer reruns the same Ansible playbook three times in a row during an incident, worried it might break something. Which statement correctly defines idempotency and explains why re-running is safe?

options.text: Running the same playbook repeatedly converges to the same desired state; if a host already matches, the run makes no changes — so re-running is safe.
Idempotency means the playbook can run only once and refuses to run a second time.
Idempotency means every run always makes changes, applying the configuration fresh each time regardless of current state.
Idempotency means the playbook automatically rolls back all changes after it finishes.

explanation: **Idempotency** means an operation can be applied many times with the same end result: the tool checks the current state and only changes what doesn't already match the desired state. If everything is already correct, a re-run reports 'ok'/no changes and is harmless — which is exactly why the engineer can safely rerun the playbook. Option (b) confuses idempotency with 'runs only once' — idempotent operations are explicitly *meant* to be re-run. Option (c) is the opposite of idempotent: always making changes regardless of state is non-idempotent and unsafe. Option (d) describes rollback, a different concept entirely. Idempotency is what makes declarative configuration management dependable.

hints.title: Converge to a target state
hints.body: Idempotent = same result no matter how many times you run it. Already-correct hosts get no changes; only drift is fixed. It is not 'run once' and not 'always change'.
